<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: integrated-reference-architecture
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/integrated-reference-architecture.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/integrated-reference-architecture.qmd@e323268f5
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Integrated Reference Architecture

After the layers have all been named, the machine has to come back together.

Intent, governance, planning, memory, routing, verification, execution,
evidence, benchmarks, policy updates, stewardship, and self-improvement cannot
remain a set of impressive diagrams. They have to compose into one inspectable
flow. A reader should be able to follow a request through the stack and answer
four questions at every handoff:

- what artifact arrived;
- what authority came with it;
- what evidence changed;
- what residual or stop condition survived.

That is the role of the integrated reference architecture. It is not a claim
that a complete ASI runtime exists. It is the trace contract a future runtime
would have to satisfy.

The happy path matters, but the blocked path matters more. A governed system
earns trust when a missing approval, inadequate context packet, failed
verification, unaccepted residual, captured evaluator, or absent rollback path
can stop the run in a way that remains visible later. If the blocked path
disappears into logs, dashboards, or prose, the architecture is not integrated.
It is only narrated.

Integration succeeds when the trace remains understandable after every layer
has done its work.

## Problem

The book has spent many chapters separating responsibilities. Authority is not
planning. Planning is not memory. Memory is not verification. Verification is
not execution. Execution is not evidence. Evidence is not policy
optimization. Policy optimization is not self-improvement permission.

That separation is necessary. It prevents a single agent loop from swallowing
every boundary into hidden prompt state.

But separation creates its own problem. A system can have a planner, a context
compiler, a router, a verifier, an execution OS, a benchmark harness, and a
steward agent, while still failing to answer whether the final action is
covered by the original authority grant. It can produce many local receipts
without producing one accountable run.

The integrated reference architecture is the answer to that problem. It asks
whether the stack can carry one request from authorized intent to artifact and
evidence-backed change without losing parentage, authority, evidence,
residuals, or stop conditions.

## Why A Layer Diagram Is Not Enough

A layer diagram can make a system look coherent before it is coherent.

Boxes can line up. Arrows can point in the right direction. A planner can hand
something to a memory system, a router can select a specialist, a verifier can
score a claim, an execution layer can produce an artifact, and a benchmark
ratchet can report pressure. But if the handoff artifacts cannot be joined
afterward, the architecture is still brittle.

The failure is architectural fragmentation. Each layer may be locally
reasonable, but the run as a whole cannot explain itself. Authority may be
ambient. Context may be unversioned. Evidence may be implied. Residuals may be
mentioned once and then lost. Benchmarks may become promotion pressure without
the old regression floor. A self-improvement candidate may inherit trust
because it arrived late in the pipeline rather than because it passed a gate.

The reference architecture therefore treats integration as a trace problem. It
does not ask whether the diagram has every modern subsystem. It asks whether a
future implementation can emit enough record structure for a skeptical reader,
reviewer, or downstream machine to reconstruct what happened and why it was
allowed.

External systems help orient the problem. Task-and-motion planning and
behavior trees name planning/execution interfaces. Distributed conditional
computation names routing and specialization pressure. TLA+, PRISM, and
runtime assurance work name formal and runtime control lanes. The ASI Stack's
specific contribution is the cross-layer handoff ledger: one record family
that keeps authority, evidence, artifacts, and residuals joined across those
lanes.

## Core Claim

The stack can be specified as an integrated reference architecture with typed
handoffs, authority stops, artifacts, evidence updates, and self-improvement
gates.

In the live book, this remains a design rationale at `argument` support.

The source notes support architecture synthesis across intent, command
contracts, plans, VCM, routing, claims, execution, benchmarks, stable
capability fields, Project Theseus, MoECOT, and report-first implementation
references. They do not show a completed integrated stack run in this
repository.

That boundary is important. A reference architecture is a specification target
for composition. It is not evidence that the composition already works.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Reference Trace Record.

A request enters through human intent and constitutional authority. Governance
turns that into a command contract or blocks it. Planning compiles the contract
into a plan DAG. Memory supplies source-bound context packets and adequacy
judgments. Routing selects capability under authority and readiness limits.
Verification checks claims, proof obligations, residuals, and route
assumptions. Execution produces work artifacts, receipts, audit logs, and
runtime results. Evidence ledgers record what changed and what did not.
Benchmark ratchets preserve floors, residuals, and pressure. Policy updates
may propose behavior-change leases. Steward agents preserve project continuity.
SCF and self-improvement gates decide whether anything is promoted, narrowed,
rolled back, quarantined, or returned for review.

At each boundary, the next layer receives a typed artifact rather than an
informal summary.

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
  participant H as Human
  participant G as Governance
  participant P as Planner
  participant M as VCM
  participant R as Router
  participant V as Verifier
  participant E as Execution
  participant L as Evidence
  participant S as SCF Gate

  H->>G: intent plus authority request
  G->>P: command contract or denial
  P->>M: plan needs and context obligations
  M->>P: source-bound context packet
  P->>R: plan DAG with capability requirements
  R->>V: route decision, limits, residuals
  V->>E: verified work order or rejection
  E->>L: artifact, receipt, audit log
  L->>S: evidence delta and regression status
  S->>G: promote, narrow, rollback, quarantine, or review
```

The trace record is not only a timeline. It carries parent artifacts,
authority deltas, evidence deltas, residual deltas, stop conditions, missing
contracts, validation commands, source references, support-state effects, and
non-claims.

Parentage says which artifact produced which next artifact. Authority deltas
say whether permission was granted, narrowed, expired, denied, escalated, or
returned for human review. Evidence deltas say whether any claim, support
state, benchmark floor, proof target, readiness gate, policy update, or release
record changed. Residual deltas say what missing context, failed verification,
contamination, safety debt, unfunded work, governance concern, or unresolved
source gap survived the handoff.

The trace turns integration into a falsifiable surface. If a layer claims to
improve the system, the trace should show what it received, what it emitted,
what authority it carried, what evidence it changed, and what later layer was
allowed to depend on it.

## Happy Paths And Blocked Paths

A happy path is not enough.

A happy path can show that the stack knows how to move forward when everything
is clean. The user intent is authorized. The plan is well formed. The context
packet is adequate. The route is allowed. The verifier accepts the work order.
Execution succeeds. The evidence ledger records a narrow update. The SCF gate
allows a scoped promotion.

That path is useful, but it is also the easiest path to fake.

The blocked path is harder and more important. Missing authority should create
a denial or escalation record. Inadequate context should block or narrow the
plan. A route with a readiness mismatch should leave a residual. A failed
proof or test should stop execution. A benchmark with contamination risk
should block promotion. A policy update with reward-hacking pressure should
remain experimental or roll back. A self-improvement candidate without
independent evaluation should not become the new evaluator.

The trace should preserve both outcomes. The architecture is governed only if
the failed path is as inspectable as the successful path.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Reference Trace Record.

It should name the trace state, execution boundary, originating intent, parent
artifacts, authority chain, layer handoffs, emitted artifacts, evidence
updates, residual deltas, stop conditions, missing contracts, validation
commands, promotion blockers, source references, support-state effects, and
non-claims.

The record gives every chapter a place in the whole stack:

- intent and governance define admissible work;
- command contracts and planning define executable structure;
- VCM defines source-bound working memory;
- routing defines which capability may act;
- claim ledgers and proof-carrying claims define evidence obligations;
- Labor OS, artifact graphs, and runtime adapters define execution receipts;
- procedural memory defines reusable work;
- benchmark ratchets define pressure and regression floors;
- policy optimization defines governed behavior change;
- artifact stewardship defines project continuity;
- SCF and self-improvement gates define promotion, rollback, quarantine, and
  review.

The record also gives future implementations a clean refusal path. A consumer
should be able to reject a trace when required artifacts, source references,
validation commands, approvals, non-claims, or rollback handles are missing.

## Invariants

No layer bypasses governance.

Artifacts remain traceable across handoffs.

Authority is preserved, narrowed, expired, denied, escalated, or returned for
review. It is never silently widened by downstream success.

Evidence deltas name exactly what changed and what did not.

Residual deltas remain visible until resolved, accepted, quarantined, or
explicitly carried forward.

Self-improvement follows evidence and authority gates.

Blank fields do not hide missing work. A missing contract, missing approval,
missing validation command, missing source reference, missing rollback path, or
missing non-claim should block, narrow, or defer the trace.

## Failure Modes

Integration theater occurs when every layer appears in a diagram but the real
run still depends on informal summaries, ambient authority, unversioned
context, unsupported evidence, or ignored stop conditions.

Trace fragmentation occurs when each layer produces a local receipt that
cannot be joined into one accountable run.

Planning/execution collapse occurs when the plan disappears into execution
and the final artifact can no longer explain which constraints, context
obligations, or authority limits shaped it.

Evidence laundering occurs when a local artifact, benchmark score, report, or
schema fixture is narrated as broader proof than the trace permits.

Self-improvement bypass occurs when a candidate change inherits trust from the
pipeline instead of passing evaluator-independence, authority, rollback,
regression, and residual gates.

Project theater is the implementation-reference version of the same failure:
dashboards, reports, work boards, or local project notes look concrete, but no
public-safe replay or digest-pinned artifact can show what happened.

The reference architecture should make these failures obvious. A broken run
should leave missing parentage, unsupported evidence deltas, unresolved
residuals, or stop conditions that were acknowledged but ignored.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a reference flow diagram, a
`reference_trace_record` schema fixture, and finite Lean predicates over
record-level handoff and governance gates.

The repository already has that first layer. The fixture validates record
shape, trace state, execution boundary, parent artifacts, authority deltas,
evidence deltas, residual deltas, promotion blockers, source references,
support-state effect, and non-claims. The Lean module proves finite
record-level predicates: a declared handoff has its required artifact, and a
trace missing a required governance gate cannot be marked valid.

Those artifacts are useful specification discipline. They do not execute the
architecture, replay a runtime trace, prove artifact continuity across a
deployed system, enforce authority stops, verify a benchmark, prove model
quality, or demonstrate deployment readiness.

The next honest implementation slice would be a tiny dry-run trace set: one
approved flow that carries stable artifact IDs from intent through plan,
context, route, work order, audit log, evidence update, and release note; and
one blocked flow that stops because authority, context adequacy,
verification, residual acceptance, or rollback is missing. That would still be
a fixture or dry run, not a deployed integrated runtime.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a trace kernel for the whole ASI Stack.

At that endpoint, every significant run emits a Reference Trace Record. The
record connects human intent, constitutional authority, command contracts,
plan DAGs, VCM packets, route decisions, claim envelopes, work orders,
execution logs, proof/spec receipts, benchmark ratchets, policy-update
leases, steward decisions, stable capability field transitions, release
records, and residual ledgers.

The product-level system would be judged by trace quality before anyone
argued about intelligence. Can it show parentage? Can it show authority
deltas? Can it show evidence deltas? Can it show residuals? Can it show why a
path was promoted, blocked, narrowed, rolled back, quarantined, or escalated?
Can downstream consumers reject a trace when a required artifact, approval,
source reference, validation command, or non-claim is missing?

The endpoint would make integration theater expensive. Informal summaries,
ambient authority, unversioned context, unsupported evidence deltas,
unaccepted residuals, ignored stop conditions, and hidden rollback gaps would
be visible defects in the trace record.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter specifies how the stack should compose. It does not claim that a
complete integrated runtime exists.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, source-note mappings, external
system-orientation records, a reference-trace schema fixture, finite Lean
record predicates, and bounded Project Theseus implementation-reference
material. The chapter's core claim stays at `argument` support.

Project Theseus is useful precisely because it is report-first: it shows how
parts of this trace could become inspectable machinery without letting local
reports stand in for public empirical proof. The current static
architecture-gate import demonstrates a report-import boundary. It is not an
end-to-end runtime trace, current dashboard state, benchmark ledger replay,
compiler proof, operator-board execution, model-quality result, or deployment
readiness claim.

The clean boundary is simple: if a future demo cannot produce parent
artifacts, authority deltas, evidence deltas, residual deltas, blocked-path
records, and no-claims, it may still be interesting, but it has not yet
instantiated this reference architecture.

## Handoff

The integrated trace defines what a complete stack must preserve. The next
chapter narrows that target into a bounded implementation reference.

Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference shows where parts of
the trace can become concrete: pressure loops, residuals, plan contracts,
gates, ledgers, operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution
governance. Its value is not that it proves the whole ASI Stack. Its value is
that it gives future work a report-first path toward public-safe replay,
digest-pinned artifacts, explicit non-claims, and eventually stronger evidence.
